SYS:
**Context: aggregate concerns - validation leg production process**

This system card is the root for the whole validation-leg build: it turns a finalized system-altitude spec into ConOps, validation rows, evidence, Jason judgment, `system_validation.json`, self-check, and close. Without the full subtree, Fizzy can demand system validation but the skill has no reliable way to produce or prove the required artifact.

The architecture spine constrains every child: AS-1 keeps `validation-rows.json` as the sole judgment store, AS-2 limits ledger writes to named locked mutators, AS-3 standardizes CLI envelopes, AS-4 protects Telegram sender attribution, AS-5 mirrors the gate, AS-6 makes digest batches idempotent, and AS-7 preserves provenance. SS-1 is Wave 0 and blocks all feature subcommands; C-4.5 depends on C-4.4 emitted bytes; C-5.3 dogfoods the completed system.
SS-1:
**Context: FM-5, FM-7, SEC-8 - ledger core and CLI contract**

This subsystem creates the shared foundation every later command depends on: the module skeleton, stdout envelope, locked atomic ledger mutation, hash canonicalization, and path/input safety. Without it, each feature command would invent its own error shape, hash behavior, and write path, recreating the split-brain and recovery failures the gauntlet found.

FM-5 drives the one-envelope/exit-code contract and status/error playbook; FM-7 drives corrupt-ledger quarantine and recovery-safe writes; SEC-8/SEC-9/SEC-10 force full hashes, path containment, row-id uniqueness, and input bounds. This is Wave 0: SS-2, SS-3, SS-4, and SS-5 all build on these primitives rather than bypassing them.
SS-2:
**Context: DD-7, CB-7, CB-10 - draft ConOps and rows without hallucinated structure**

This subsystem owns the Phase 7 drafting path: derive ConOps from the roadmap, normalize draft rows, and lint them before implementation begins. Without it, conductors hand-write hashes, accidentally create phantom `US-n` obligations, or draft rows that look valid locally but fail coverage or anti-relabeling later.

DD-7 makes ConOps deterministic and manifest-bound; CB-7 requires the module, not the LLM, to stamp row hashes; CB-10 and CB-11 force exact story coverage and stray-id detection; SEC-7 keeps validation from being relabeled verification. SS-2 depends on SS-1's ledger/hash/path contracts and feeds SS-3 evidence plus SS-4 projection.
SS-3:
**Context: FM-2, RC-2, SEC-4 - evidence and digest delivery path**

This subsystem turns completed implementation work into reviewable validation evidence and a digest Jason can actually judge. Without it, evidence hashes are brittle markdown, digests can include stale or unseen rows, and mobile review can fail because payloads are too large, unsafe, or missing context.

FM-2 drives module-stamped evidence front matter; DD-1 keeps digest assembly separate from reset/remediation; RC-2 requires per-part delivery before bulk verdicts; SEC-4 and FM-9 shape secret linting, escaping, and byte budgets. It depends on SS-1 ledger primitives and produces the sent batch that SS-4 reply parsing is allowed to judge.
SS-4:
**Context: CB-9, SEC-1, RC-1 - judgment application and close safety**

This subsystem owns the high-risk close path: parse Jason replies, verify sender identity, mutate judgments, supersede/reset rows, emit `system_validation.json`, self-check it, and report status. Without it, the conductor could apply the wrong message, accept ambiguous replies, skip required self-checks, or project a stale ledger into the Fizzy gate.

CB-9 and FM-10 constrain reply grammar and justifications; SEC-1/SEC-2 move trust from conductor-typed sender flags to raw Telegram payload parsing; RC-1 and CB-5 force self-check on exact emitted bytes immediately before MCP close. C-4.5 depends on C-4.4; C-4.6 gives operators the read-only state needed for re-entry.
SS-5:
**Context: DD-2, FM-8, ACK-4 - phase wiring and dogfood proof**

This subsystem makes the process operable by future conductors, not just implemented in code. Without the Phase 7 and Phase 8 docs, agents will not know when to derive rows, how to run the deterministic close algorithm, or how to respond to local/gate errors; without dogfood, the circular bootstrap risk remains only asserted.

DD-2 demands one close algorithm instead of scattered prose; FM-8 makes MCP close preflight and read-back concrete; OP-10 moves timing and human-gated checks into dogfood metrics. SS-5 depends on real subcommand names from SS-2/3/4, and C-5.3 depends on every implementation and doc card.
C-1-1:
**Context: FM-5, OP-11 - module skeleton and stdout envelope**

This card prevents every subcommand from inventing its own interface. FM-5 found no status command, no local error playbook, and no defined stdout shape; without a shared skeleton, downstream tests cannot distinguish user errors, transient failures, and successful envelopes consistently.

The fix is an argparse module with all 13 subcommands wired to one JSON envelope and the 0/2/3 exit-code contract. OP-3/OP-4 add UTC timestamps and schema/module version stamping; OP-11 keeps enforcement labels honest. This is SS-1 foundation work, so every later command must use this boundary rather than print bespoke output.
C-1-2:
**Context: FM-7, US-8, DD-6 - locked atomic ledger mutation**

This card protects the single ledger from partial writes, concurrent mutation, and corrupt-state recovery loss. FM-7 warned that restoring from git can lose uncommitted judgments; US-8 and DIS-1 shaped the practical filelock timeout and stale-lock policy; DD-6 requires provenance on artifacts that survive recovery.

The fix is one mutation helper that reads, mutates, and atomic-renames under `validation-rows.json.lock`, quarantines corrupt bytes before returning `LEDGER_CORRUPT`, and reports `LEDGER_BUSY` with useful diagnostics. All feature mutators depend on this helper; read-only commands must have no write path.
C-1-3:
**Context: SEC-8, DD-5, RC-3 - canonical hashes that survive review**

This card prevents hash drift and hand-authored hex from undermining validation evidence. SEC-8 requires full hashes locally with 12-hex prefixes only at artifact boundaries; DD-5 requires judgment history to record hash context; RC-3 requires batches to snapshot ConOps state before replies are accepted.

The fix pins one canonical `row_hash` formula, NFC normalization, full conops/story hashes, and tests for what does and does not affect a row hash. SS-2 normalization, SS-3 evidence, SS-4 projection, and C-4.5 self-check all rely on this exact canonicalization to detect stale rows instead of guessing.
C-1-4:
**Context: SEC-9, SEC-10 - path containment and input bounds**

This card closes the simple ways a local CLI can read or write the wrong artifact. SEC-9 requires realpath containment under the spec root, symlink rejection, duplicate story detection, and globally unique row IDs; SEC-10 requires deterministic size caps for replies, justifications, ledgers, and ConOps files.

The fix is shared path and bounds validation in SS-1, before feature commands touch data. That shape keeps later commands focused on domain rules while still failing closed on traversal, oversized input, or invalid IDs. SS-2/3/4 depend on these checks for every artifact path they consume or emit.
C-2-1:
**Context: DD-7, CB-11, FM-3 - deterministic ConOps derivation**

This card prevents the ConOps file from becoming free-form prose that drifts from the roadmap or mints phantom gate obligations. DD-7 requires deterministic inputs from manifest story fields and milestone context only; CB-11 requires stray `US-n` lint because the gate scans story IDs by regex; FM-3 requires per-story hashes so one story edit does not orphan all evidence.

The fix is a templated `derive-conops` command that refuses unsafe overwrite unless forced when rows reference the old hash. It depends on SS-1 path/hash behavior and feeds C-2.2 row normalization plus every later evidence and projection check.
C-2-2:
**Context: CB-7, DD-4 - normalize rows as the hash producer**

This card removes the impossible requirement that an LLM conductor hand-compute canonical sha256 values. CB-7 found row hashes had no producer; DD-4 found active and superseded rows needed a formal predicate and full snapshots rather than audit stubs.

The fix is a mutating `normalize-rows` command that stamps schema fields, row hashes, story hashes, row IDs, and global uniqueness under the SS-1 lock. It is the bridge between human-drafted row prose and machine-checkable ledger state. C-2.3 lint, C-3.1 evidence, C-3.2 digest snapshots, and C-4.4 projection all depend on normalized rows.
C-2-3:
**Context: CB-10, SEC-7, DD-8 - row coverage and oracle lint**

This card catches rows that would satisfy the wrong thing. CB-10 found gate substring matching can let `US-1` accidentally satisfy `US-11`; SEC-7 found anti-relabeling can be bypassed by omitting or overlapping verification targets; DD-8 requires draft mode so incremental row drafting is not blocked by full coverage too early.

The fix is structural lint: exact one-story `conops_ref`, coverage checks, oracle form checks, banned verification-success phrases, and test-target overlap rules. It depends on C-2.1 ConOps and C-2.2 normalized hashes, then gives SS-3/SS-4 a clean ledger to execute and close.
C-3-1:
**Context: FM-2, FM-4, DD-9 - evidence with durable provenance**

This card prevents evidence from being loose markdown that can be stale, malformed, or disconnected from the implementation being validated. FM-2 requires canonical front matter produced by the module; FM-4 requires reset failures to invalidate old evidence; DD-9 requires binding evidence to implementation commit and clean-worktree state.

The fix is `record-evidence`: scaffold and validate exact front matter, stamp row/story/ConOps hashes, store the conductor's mobile-sufficient summary, and mutate the ledger under lock. C-3.2 refuses digest assembly until these evidence artifacts are present and hash-consistent.
C-3-2:
**Context: DD-1, RC-3, SEC-4 - digest assembly without hidden mutation**

This card keeps digest creation pure and review-safe. DD-1 split reset logic out of assembly; RC-3 requires ConOps and row hash snapshots at batch open; SEC-4 requires secret linting and escaping so row prose cannot spoof digest instructions; FM-9 and OP-9 constrain payload size in UTF-8 bytes.

The fix is `assemble-digest`: gather only active unjudged rows with valid evidence, snapshot hashes, write bounded part files under `validation-digests/`, and refuse if a non-terminal batch already exists. C-3.3 records delivery of those exact parts before SS-4 can accept bulk verdicts.
C-3-3:
**Context: RC-2, FM-6, FM-12 - delivery tracking before judgment**

This card closes the biggest flow hole: Jason could say `pass all` before all rows were actually delivered. RC-2 requires batch states from assembled to sent to closed/cancelled with per-part message IDs; FM-6 defines send/listen failure behavior; FM-12 defines what humans see when an already-sent batch is cancelled.

The fix is `record-send` plus `cancel-batch`: record every part outcome, mark a batch sent only when all parts are sent, return rows to the delta pool on cancellation, and audit notices. C-4.1 parse-reply depends on this state to reject premature bulk verdicts.
C-4-1:
**Context: CB-9, FM-10, RC-4 - unambiguous reply parsing**

This card prevents ambiguous or duplicated human replies from mutating the ledger. CB-9 found ordering, duplicate, continuation-line, and bulk-verdict ambiguities; FM-10 requires fail justifications because remediation cards depend on them; RC-4 requires idempotency by `reply_ref` and ignoring edited Telegram messages.

The fix is a deterministic grammar: explicit per-row verdicts win, `fail rest` and `na rest` reject, fixed natural-language bulk-pass aliases are allowed, and invalid replies apply zero mutations with a structured re-prompt. It depends on C-3.3 sent batch state and owns the locked judgment mutation for SS-4.
C-4-2:
**Context: SEC-1, SEC-2, OP-1 - Telegram sender trust boundary**

This card prevents the conductor from accidentally authenticating the wrong message by typing `--sender-id` itself. SEC-1 right-sizes the threat as agent confusion, not account takeover; SEC-2 separates chat ID from allowed sender IDs; OP-1 requires durable security events for discarded replies.

The fix is for `parse-reply --source telegram` to read the raw wake-listener update file, extract sender/message/text itself, compare against registry `allowed_sender_ids`, and fail closed on malformed config. Non-allowlisted replies mutate nothing and record a hashed-sender event. C-4.1 uses this identity before applying grammar results.
C-4-3:
**Context: CB-1, FM-1, FM-4 - reset and supersession paths**

This card gives failed or stale validation rows a legal recovery path. CB-1 found the state table contradicted the invariant that judged-pass rows exit only through supersession; FM-1 clarified that remediation cards are conductor-created and module-recorded assertions; FM-4 requires fresh evidence after reset.

The fix separates `reset-failed` from `supersede-row`: unchanged failed scenarios reset with a required remediation reference and invalidated evidence, while changed scenarios or mistaken passes supersede from any state with Jason approval and full snapshot audit. C-3.1 then records new evidence; C-4.4 projects only active rows.
C-4-4:
**Context: CB-2, CB-3, DD-3 - emit the gate artifact from ledger state**

This card prevents hand-transcribed or split-brain `system_validation.json`. CB-2 requires `result == null` iff `judgment == null`; CB-3 requires complete batch lifecycle fields; CB-12 defines `na` to `not-applicable`; DD-3 keeps one rich artifact unless parity testing disproves gate tolerance.

The fix is a read-only projection from `validation-rows.json` into the gate artifact, preserving test targets, result enums, row order, provenance, story freshness, and a ledger hash binding. The non-literal `PARA-ledger-hash` plan note is handled here as the exact-ledger-state binding. C-4.5 self-check runs on these emitted bytes.
C-4-5:
**Context: RC-1, SEC-7, CB-5 - self-check immediately before MCP close**

This card catches local artifact mistakes before the Fizzy gate sees them. RC-1 found a TOCTOU gap between self-check and MCP; SEC-7 requires anti-relabeling parity; CB-5 says re-entry with an existing artifact must still self-check immediately before close, and failed rows must route to remediation instead of emission shortcuts.

The fix mirrors all eight gate reject classes, adds stricter local overlap checks, emits and re-verifies the exact artifact sha256, and requires verification-ledger context when verification artifacts exist. It depends on C-4.4's emitted bytes and is the final local guard before `mark_system_validation_complete`.
C-4-6:
**Context: FM-5, OP-7, OP-8 - read-only status for re-entry**

This card is not a core mutator; it gives operators and future agents the state they need to recover safely. FM-5 required a status command and local error playbook, OP-7 required stale open batch visibility, and OP-8 requires retention rather than cleanup during the session.

The fix is a read-only reporter whose proof obligation is that ledger bytes do not change. It reports active batch age, per-part send state, unjudged rows, judgment summary, coverage state, blockers, and the next close step. SS-4 and SS-5 use this for re-entry and dogfood diagnostics without opening another write path.
C-5-1:
**Context: US-0, CB-7, OP-11 - Phase 7 validation-leg instructions**

This card makes the drafting process discoverable at the moment conductors need it. US-0 is a roadmap bootstrap story rather than a gauntlet concern, but it is the reason this doc task exists: a fresh conductor must know how to derive ConOps, draft rows, normalize hashes, and check rows before implementation starts.

CB-7 shapes the sequence so the conductor drafts prose but the module computes hashes; DD-10 explains where Jason's pre-judgment veto fits; OP-11 requires honest enforcement labels. It depends on SS-2 command names and feeds C-5.3's cold-read dogfood metric.
C-5-2:
**Context: DD-2, FM-8, CB-5 - Phase 8 close algorithm**

This card prevents the close path from remaining scattered across prose and memory. DD-2 requires one deterministic re-entrant algorithm; FM-8 requires concrete MCP preflight, metadata read-back, retry, and lost-response handling; CB-5 requires self-check before MCP even when an artifact already exists.

The fix is a Phase 8 validation-leg section with the close algorithm, all eight gate reject responses, local error-code responses, fail-row routing, and `NOTHING_TO_DIGEST` handling. It depends on real SS-3/SS-4 subcommands and supports C-5.3 dogfood plus future system-altitude sessions.
C-5-3:
**Context: ACK-4, OP-10, US-10 - dogfood the validation leg on this session**

This card proves the new process can close the very session that introduced it. ACK-4 acknowledges the bootstrap circularity; OP-10 separates deterministic CI from human-gated dogfood and turns the 30-minute claim into a measured acceptance metric; US-10 is a roadmap story rather than a gauntlet concern.

The fix is manual dogfood after all implementation, docs, and verification are done: run the validation leg, collect Jason's intent-level judgments, record retries/re-prompts/re-emissions, and confirm the Fizzy metadata advances. It depends on every other card because it validates the integrated system, not an isolated command.
